speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Your hemoglobin level today is 159 g/L — that’s well within the normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose is 7.7 mmol/L — slightly elevated, but not alarming at this time. Are you monitoring it regularly at home?

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 135 mmHg — borderline high, but consistent with your diagnosis of essential hypertension.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been checking my blood sugar twice a day since my last visit. It’s usually between 6.8 and 7.5 — though yesterday it was higher after lunch.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for that detail. Let’s also review your current medications — you’re on Lisinopril 20 mg once daily, correct?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — Lisinopril 20 mg once daily is what we started last month to manage your blood pressure.

speaker: patient
text: That’s right. I haven’t missed a dose, but I did notice a bit of dry cough lately — nothing severe, but it’s persistent.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is 37.4 °C — completely normal. No signs of acute infection today.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your ongoing hypertension and the new symptoms, your primary diagnosis remains essential hypertension.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also felt a little more fatigued this week, and I had a low-grade fever yesterday evening — around 37.8, I think.

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, just now your temperature registered 38.4 °C — that suggests a mild febrile response. Let’s keep an eye on that.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the persistent cough and now the fever, I’m going to switch you from Lisinopril to Amlodipine 10 mg once daily — it’s equally effective for blood pressure control and won’t cause that cough.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that today. Should I stop the Lisinopril cold turkey or taper it?

speaker: doctor
text: You can discontinue Lisinopril today — no taper needed. We’ll monitor your BP closely over the next week.

speaker: doctor
text: Your repeat hemoglobin came back at 148 g/L — a modest drop from the 159 we saw earlier this morning. We’ll recheck in a week to see if it stabilizes.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been feeling lightheaded a couple of times when standing up quickly — could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s possible — orthostatic symptoms can occur with BP fluctuations or medication changes. Let’s check your sitting and standing pressures now.

speaker: patient
text: I checked my BP at home this morning before coming in — it was lower than usual.

speaker: doctor
text: Indeed — your systolic pressure is now 106 mmHg, which is notably lower than the 135 we recorded earlier. That may explain the lightheadedness.

speaker: patient
text: Right — that 135 reading you took at the start is the one I was thinking of when I said my home readings felt off compared to that number.
